subject: Infectious Disease
question: A 35-year-old immigrant from India presents with 2 months of productive cough, night sweats, weight loss, and hemoptysis. CXR shows right upper lobe cavitary lesion. Sputum AFB positive. What is the standard initial treatment regimen?

options.text: RIPE: Rifampin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol for 2 months, then RI for 4 months
options.explanation: Standard 6-month regimen: 2 months RIPE (4 drugs), then 4 months RI (2 drugs). Pyridoxine (B6) given with INH to prevent neuropathy. DOT recommended. Check LFTs monthly.
9-month RI regimen is for LATENT TB (prophylaxis), not active pulmonary TB. Active TB requires 4-drug initial therapy to prevent resistance.
Fluoroquinolones are second-line TB drugs (MDR-TB). Not first-line. Community-acquired pneumonia antibiotics are ineffective against TB. Quinolone monotherapy breeds resistance.
INH monotherapy is for latent TB only. Active TB requires multi-drug therapy. Monotherapy rapidly breeds resistance.
Streptomycin is an injectable second-line agent for MDR-TB. Not first-line due to ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity. RIPE is standard initial therapy.

educationalObjective: Treat active pulmonary TB with RIPE regimen: 2 months of 4 drugs, then 4 months of 2 drugs. Always use directly observed therapy.
keyTakeaway: Active TB: RIPE x 2mo → RI x 4mo. Pyridoxine with INH. Check LFTs. DOT recommended. Latent TB: Rifampin x 4mo or INH x 9mo.
learning_objective: Master this concept in Infectious Disease
